skill.name: api-documentation
skill.description: Generate API references, onboarding guides, examples, and interactive documentation from OpenAPI specs and code comments.
skill.summary: Create API documentation packages with references, examples, authentication notes, and interactive tooling guidance.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, credential, and weak-cryptography patterns in SKILL.md. Review shows the weak-cryptography and env-access matches are false positives from documentation text and placeholder API keys, while command and network examples are legitimate but require user review before execution. No evidence found of prompt injection, hidden code, malicious exfiltration, or autonomous execution.

medium_findings.title: Executable Documentation Tooling Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill includes shell examples for package installation, project creation, repository cloning, local servers, builds, and ReadMe.io uploads. These commands are relevant to API documentation work, but users should review package sources, working directories, and upload targets before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly visible in documentation examples and would execute external tooling if copied. The context is legitimate documentation generation, so this is a controlled medium risk rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Network and Credential-Bearing Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill shows external CDN loading, API requests with authorization placeholders, and ReadMe.io commands that accept an API key. The examples use placeholders and common services, but they can transmit API specs or credentials if users replace placeholders without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network examples and API-key placeholders are explicit. I did not find evidence of hidden exfiltration or real embedded secrets, so the risk is about user-directed data transmission.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography findings match ordinary documentation terms such as description, Markdown, and HTTP status documentation. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic implementation in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain prose and Markdown structure, not cryptographic APIs. The repeated matches are consistent with substring-based static analysis noise.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Matches Are Placeholder Credentials
low_findings.description: The env_access findings point to Authorization headers and CLI examples that use placeholder values like YOUR_API_KEY and YOUR_README_API_KEY. No evidence found of reading environment variables or accessing local secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence consists of static placeholder strings in documentation examples. There is no process.env, shell environment expansion, or file-based secret access in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Matches Are API Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance flags align with example HTTP status tables, client request snippets, and localhost documentation servers. No evidence found of host enumeration, port scanning, or network discovery intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious lines are static examples for API reference content and language clients. They do not perform discovery or inspect the user system.

dangerous_patterns.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed After Context Review
dangerous_patterns.description: The analyzer combined command examples, network examples, and credential placeholders into a critical heuristic. Manual review found a single Markdown instruction file with examples only, no scripts, no hidden payloads, and no autonomous credential access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky patterns are present, but their semantic context is educational API documentation content. The absence of executable skill files lowers confidence in any malicious interpretation.

content.value_statement: API teams need accurate references, examples, and onboarding guides. This skill helps generate structured API documentation from specs, code comments, and common documentation tooling.
actual_capabilities: Creates API reference structures with endpoints, parameters, responses, status codes, and examples.
Explains setup patterns for Swagger UI, Redoc, Stoplight Elements, Docusaurus, Slate, ReadMe.io, and MkDocs.
Generates getting started guides, authentication sections, pagination notes, rate limits, and error handling documentation.
Shows how to derive documentation from OpenAPI comments, TypeScript types, and FastAPI docstrings.
Provides multi-language request examples for curl, JavaScript, Python, Go, and Ruby.
Outlines export paths for Markdown, HTML, Postman collections, and SDK-oriented documentation.
limitations: It is a Markdown guide, not an executable generator with built-in parsing logic.
Generated examples require validation against the real API before publication.
Interactive documentation setup can require installing third-party packages and running local servers.
Publishing to services such as ReadMe.io requires user-managed credentials and access controls.

use_cases.title: Publish a New API Reference
use_cases.description: Create endpoint documentation with parameters, authentication details, responses, errors, and sample requests.

use_cases.title: Improve Developer Onboarding
use_cases.description: Draft getting started guides, first request flows, code examples, and troubleshooting notes for external developers.

use_cases.title: Standardize Internal API Docs
use_cases.description: Convert scattered code comments and OpenAPI details into consistent Markdown or interactive documentation sections.

prompt_templates.title: Document One Endpoint
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner task for a single route
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to document the GET /users endpoint. Include purpose, parameters, authentication, success response, error cases, and one request example.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Getting Started Guide
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to write a getting started guide for my API. Cover prerequisites, API key setup, first request, error handling, and next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Generate OpenAPI-Based Docs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to turn my OpenAPI summary into API reference sections. Group endpoints by tag and include schemas, examples, pagination, and rate limits.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Interactive Docs Portal
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan an interactive API documentation portal. Compare Swagger UI, Redoc, Stoplight Elements, and static-site options for my release workflow.

output_examples.input: A request to document a users endpoint.
output_examples.output: An API reference section with endpoint purpose, authentication requirements, query parameters, response fields, status codes, and concise request examples.

output_examples.input: A request to create developer onboarding material.
output_examples.output: A getting started guide that explains account setup, credential handling, the first API request, expected responses, and links to deeper reference pages.

output_examples.input: A request to choose an interactive documentation stack.
output_examples.output: A comparison of Swagger UI, Redoc, Stoplight Elements, and static documentation tools with setup notes and publishing considerations.

best_practices: Validate every generated endpoint, schema, and status code against the current API implementation.
Use placeholder credentials in examples and document secure secret handling separately.
Test copied commands in a sandbox before adding them to production documentation workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not publish generated examples without checking real request and response behavior.
Do not include real API keys, bearer tokens, passwords, or customer data in documentation examples.
Do not run package installs, repository clones, or upload commands without reviewing their source and destination.

faq.question: Can this skill generate OpenAPI documentation?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides patterns for creating API references from OpenAPI specs and code comments.

faq.question: Does this skill run a documentation server automatically?
faq.answer: No. It shows commands and setup examples, but users must run and review commands themselves.

faq.question: Can it create interactive API docs?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains common setup patterns for Swagger UI, Redoc, and Stoplight Elements.

faq.question: Is it safe to include API keys in generated examples?
faq.answer: No. Use placeholders only, and keep real credentials out of documentation files.

faq.question: Can it help with SDK documentation?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes SDK generation concepts and multi-language request example patterns.

faq.question: What should users review before publishing?
faq.answer: Review endpoints, schemas, examples, authentication text, rate limits, error codes, and any third-party publishing commands.
